blocks.text: The word of the Lord that came to Zephaniah son of Cushi son of Gedaliah son of Amariah son of Hezekiah, in the days of King Josiah son of Amon of Judah.

1.1: Superscription. may be either a name or an ethnic designation, “Ethiopian” (see 2.12 and 3.10). If is the eighth-century reformer King Hezekiah (727/715–698/687 ; 2 Kings 18–20), then Zephaniah was a member of the royal family, which may be indicated by the unusually long genealogy. (640–609 ) was also a reformer (2 Kings 22–23), although his father had sacrificed to carved images (2 Chr 33.22).
I will utterly sweep away everything from the face of the earth, says the Lord.

I will sweep away humans and animals; I will sweep away the birds of the air and the fish of the sea. I will make the wicked stumble. I will cut off humanity from the face of the earth, says the Lord.

2–3 : Punishment is announced against on . The language echoes that used in the creation and flood narratives (Gen 1; 6.7; 7.4 and 8.8): (lit., “all”); humans (Heb “ʾadam”); (Heb “ʾadamah”). God's announcement returns the earth to the destruction announced in Gen 6–7 and overturns the divine promise in Gen 8–9 never again to destroy the earth.
I will stretch out my hand against Judah, and against all the inhabitants of Jerusalem; and I will cut off from this place every remnant of Baal and the name of the idolatrous priests;
those who bow down on the roofs to the host of the heavens; those who bow down and swear to the Lord, but also swear by Milcom;

4–5 : The language of f continues, as God charges Judah's inhabitants with combining worship of the with worship of other deities such as the Canaanite god , the astral deities known as , and the Ammonite god These charges, as well as the term for , fit well with the accounts of Manasseh's idolatry in 2 Kings 21 and Josiah's reforms in 2 Kings 23; see also Jer 19.13; 32.29.
5 : Repetition within v. 5 underscores the duplicity of the people: they and both to the host of heaven and to Yahweh.
those who have turned back from following the Lord, who have not sought the Lord or inquired of him.
Be silent before the Lord God! For the day of the Lord is at hand; the Lord has prepared a sacrifice, he has consecrated his guests.
7 : , cf. Hab 2.20. Though court officials might be expected to be the at the 's sacrificial banquet, in an ironic turn they are described as the itself.
And on the day of the Lord's sacrifice I will punish the officials and the king's sons and all who dress themselves in foreign attire.
On that day I will punish all who leap over the threshold, who fill their master's house with violence and fraud.
8–9 : The linkage between those who adopt with those who , likely a non-Judean religious custom (cf. 1 Sam 5.5), connects assimilation with idolatry.
On that day, says the Lord, a cry will be heard from the Fish Gate, a wail from the Second Quarter, a loud crash from the hills.
The inhabitants of the Mortar wail, for all the traders have perished; all who weigh out silver are cut off.
10–11 : (Neh 3.3; 12.39), (2 Kings 22.14), and (unidentified) likely all refer to wealthy districts in Jerusalem, where work.
At that time I will search Jerusalem with lamps, and I will punish the people who rest complacently on their dregs, those who say in their hearts, “The Lord will not do good, nor will he do harm.”
Their wealth shall be plundered, and their houses laid waste. Though they build houses, they shall not inhabit them; though they plant vineyards, they shall not drink wine from them.
7–13 : The implications of the for Judah.
8–13 : A listing of Judah's crimes.

12–13 : Like the fourth-century Greek philosopher Diogenes who roamed Athens with a lamp seeking an honest man, the will search Jerusalem with lamps to find the guilty. Because they doubt God's oversight of the world (see also Mal 2.17), their labor will not be successful (see Deut 28.30–31 and cf. Isa 65.21).
The great day of the Lord is near, near and hastening fast; the sound of the day of the Lord is bitter, the warrior cries aloud there.
That day will be a day of wrath, a day of distress and anguish, a day of ruin and devastation, a day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness,

15 : The term appears six times in this verse; “Dies irae,” the Vulgate translation of , is the title of the well-known medieval sequence for the dead; translates Heb “shoʾah,” a term today used for the Holocaust. Compare Am 5.18–20.
a day of trumpet blast and battle cry against the fortified cities and against the lofty battlements.
I will bring such distress upon people that they shall walk like the blind; because they have sinned against the Lord, their blood shall be poured out like dust, and their flesh like dung.

Neither their silver nor their gold will be able to save them on the day of the Lord's wrath; in the fire of his passion the whole earth shall be consumed; for a full, a terrible end he will make of all the inhabitants of the earth.
1.2–18: The coming day of the Lord.
7–18 : (Joel 1.15; 3.14; Am 5.18; Ob 15; Zech 12.4). Verse 7 introduces vocabulary that will dominate this unit: appears thirteen times through the end of the chapter.

14–18 : The chapter ends as it began, with attention on (v. 18). As elsewhere in the prophets, the is one of divine (Isa 13.9) from which and provide no protection (Ezek 7.19). The sins of Judah lead to the destruction of all creation.
